new_feedback.issue: The page renders all three states stacked under explanatory copy ('Three states are shown below...'). This reads as a marketing comp or design-system page, not the operator surface a model risk officer would open to launch a run. A bank examiner reviewing the audit surface would not see one composite of three states; they would see the live route. Composite layouts erode the credibility that this is the actual governance tool rather than a sales mockup.
new_feedback.change: Remove the slice masthead, the three colored state-header chips, and the descriptive paragraph. Show ONE state per artifact (three separate files or three tabs in a state-switcher), so the screenshot in evidence is indistinguishable from the live route at /. If a state-comparison view is needed for design review, it should not be a deliverable layout.

new_feedback.issue: Two dollar metrics persist with no timestamp, no run count denominator, and no link to the underlying ledger. SR 11-7 expects every cost figure tied to a model run to be traceable to the invoice line, model_id, and timestamp. A floating '$61.40' for SESSION is exactly the 'single number with no context' that fails the trust test.
new_feedback.change: Add an 'as of HH:MM:SSZ' timestamp under each chip, make the chip a click target labeled with a small caret or 'view ledger' affordance, and label SESSION with the run count it covers (e.g. 'SESSION · 7 runs · $61.40'). EST/RUN needs a tooltip or footnote naming the cost-model version that produced the estimate.

new_feedback.issue: The amber callout warns that the LLM judge is 'gameable by an adversary that knows the scheme.' For a model risk officer this is the right disclosure, but it is buried as a soft note in the summary rail. SR 11-7 effective-challenge documentation requires the limitation to be acknowledged BEFORE start, and the operator's acknowledgment to be recorded. As drawn, the operator can click Start without ever interacting with this warning.
new_feedback.change: Promote the half-vote disclosure to a required acknowledgment checkbox above the Start button ('I acknowledge the LLM judge carries one of five votes and is adversarially gameable'). The checkbox state must be part of the run-launch payload so the audit log shows acknowledgment was given for this run.

new_feedback.element: Sealed-spec YAML block (lines 114-129) and target-picker cards (lines 76-99)
new_feedback.issue: The spec preview shows obligations and oracles, but there is no spec hash, no spec version, no author, no created-at timestamp, no link to the prior approved spec for this target. The target-picker cards show no model_id, no last-validated date, no model owner. A bank examiner asking 'which exact spec ran' or 'which version of the Fraud adapter was attacked' cannot answer from this surface.
new_feedback.change: Under the spec file label, add 'sha256: <8-char>… · v3.2.1 · author: <user> · created 2026-06-21 14:02Z'. Under each target card, add a small monospace strip with 'model_id · last validated YYYY-MM-DD · owner: <team>'. Make the sha and model_id clickable to provenance.

new_feedback.issue: Dollar ceiling is editable free-text with no displayed cap, no approver, no policy reference. A model risk officer needs to see the workspace's policy ceiling and who can approve overrides — otherwise the budget is operator discretion, which fails segregation-of-duties review. The footnote '≈ $0.52 per round · hard stop at $25.00' also lacks a citation to the cost-model that produced $0.52.
new_feedback.change: Show 'workspace ceiling: $X (policy ref MRG-12.3)' adjacent to the dollar-ceiling input; visually distinguish operator entry from policy ceiling. If the entered value exceeds policy, surface an inline 'requires risk approver' affordance rather than allowing free entry. Link '$0.52 per round' to the cost-model card or commit.

new_feedback.element: Page background and gridless layout overall
new_feedback.issue: The body is a single dark panel with no gridlines, no row striping, no column rulers. Bloomberg, internal model-governance portals, and Excel risk dashboards all carry visible grid structure that lets a risk officer scan and reference cells. The current layout reads closer to a developer settings page than an auditable form.
new_feedback.change: Add a subtle 1px grid divider between each form section (target / spec / budget) and a labeled column ruler on the right rail (e.g. tick marks for 0/25/50/75/100% of budget consumed on the EST / RUN chip). Even minimal grid scaffolding signals 'this is a governance surface' rather than 'this is a marketing form.'

new_feedback.element: Error state 'Spec was not sealed — validation stopped at line 6, column 5' bar (lines 330-334)
new_feedback.issue: The error tells the operator where parsing failed but does NOT tell the audit log what was attempted. For SR 11-7 a failed validation is itself a recordable event — the bar should reference the attempt ID, the validator model/version that ran, and where the audit entry lives.
new_feedback.change: Extend the error bar with a monospace tail: 'attempt id: a3f9c2… · validator: spec-validator-v1.4 · audit entry: /audit/attempts/a3f9c2 ↗'. Make the audit-entry path a real link affordance.

new_feedback.element: Inspect drawer footer 'API keys, DB credentials, and sandbox tokens are redacted — the only values ever hidden.' (lines 470-473)
new_feedback.issue: This is the right SR 11-7 posture but it is a single sentence in a 12px muted color. A risk officer skim-reading the drawer for an audit will miss the redaction policy. The redaction guarantee is exactly the kind of claim an examiner will ask the bank to defend in writing.
new_feedback.change: Promote the redaction notice to a small bordered chip at the TOP of the drawer body, not the bottom: 'REDACTION POLICY · only secrets are hidden · policy ref MRG-7.1 ↗'. Make the policy ref clickable. The drawer's whole credibility rests on this guarantee being prominent.
